五级递进控制器课程,全部跑在真实硬件上:PID、LQR、滑模控制、增益调度、主动容错,四轴无人机与固定翼并行贯穿始终。
建立飞行控制的第一直觉:在固定翼和四旋翼两种机体上分别设计并调试级联 PID 姿态控制器,看清楚这两条路径到达同一个结论的方式其实截然不同。
用 AVL 从 FT-1 的真实几何建立气动模型,在 Simulink 里配平并线性化。在信任这个线性化结果之前,用第二种独立方法交叉验证——这一级里的每一个控制器都建立在这个模型之上。
设计一个直接的纯姿态 PID——俯仰和滚转分别直接闭环到升降舵/副翼——用 pidtune 在线性模型上调参。大部分时候它能工作,直到你仔细看:某个轴在每次指令之后要振荡几十秒都稳不下来,不管怎么重调那一个增益都解决不了。
升级为角速度+姿态级联双环
搞清楚为什么单纯重调单环增益解决不了这个振荡:它的修正信号要多经过一次积分才能作用到飞机真正的角速度上,这个额外的相位滞后是在激发这个轻阻尼模态,而不是在抑制它。把它重新搭成内环角速度+外环姿态的级联结构,稳态误差和振荡幅度都会收敛到指令的百分之几以内。
用非线性飞机测试这个线性设计
这套级联控制器完全是在一个小信号线性模型上设计出来的。把同一组增益接到 FT-1 完整的非线性动力学上闭环跑一遍,看看线性化做的那些假设,到底能保住多少干净的性能。
带着失效的舵面继续飞
在机动过程中注入升降舵部分失效或副翼卡死,记录这套从没被设计去检测故障的经典 PID 级联控制器会做出什么反应。这是之后每一级容错设计都要拿来对比的基线。
从台架实测的推力/力矩系数出发,建立刚体+电机模型——这里用不上气动面板法,四旋翼的模型直接来自对硬件本身的标定。在信任它之前,先用真实推力台架数据验证。
设计内环角速度 PID。把带宽、超调、稳态误差变成能亲手调出来的飞行语汇,而不是课本上的抽象名词。
四旋翼没有固定翼那种“先试试纯姿态、发现效果差”的奢侈条件——没有气动自阻尼,一个底下没有角速度反馈的姿态环,带来的不是曲线上的振荡,而是真实的摔机风险。先靠分析想清楚为什么,再上真实硬件搭外环姿态控制,而且是带着已经想清楚的理由去搭。
用非线性机体测试这个线性设计
这套级联控制器是在小角度线性化上调出来的。把它推进大角度机动,让线性模型忽略掉的欧拉角奇异性和陀螺耦合非线性开始起作用,看看真实的裕度到底在哪里。
现场注入单电机推力衰减 60% 与 IMU 噪声突增。精确记录 PID 能撑到哪个边界才失稳——也为 L3/L5 埋下伏笔。

Robavionix 平台上每一级课程都跑在同一套标准故障库上。L1 是这一切开始的地方:一个手动调试的级联 PID 姿态控制器,在两种物理上截然不同的机体上分别搭建,各自以自己的方式被推到极限、失效。

## 为什么从这里开始

控制类教学的失败,往往不在内容深度,而在学生某一级数学台阶没跨过去就被拖着走。L1 只假设传递函数与频域分析——没有状态空间,没有 Lyapunov 函数。目标是第一堂课就在两种机体上都跑起一个能工作的控制器,并且精确地、亲身地回答一个问题——而且这个问题的答案在两种机体上其实并不一样:**PID 控制器到底能被推到多远才会放弃?这个极限,在四旋翼上和在固定翼上,是同一个极限吗?**

## 你将搭建什么

四旋翼这边:每个轴上的角速度 PID,再叠加外环姿态控制,烧录到 STM32 飞控板上,在 HIL 实验台的真实四轴机体上运行——不是纯仿真。FT-1 这边:同样的角速度+姿态级联结构,但设计对象是一个先用两种独立方法交叉验证过、再拿去调参的 AVL/Simulink 气动模型,调完还要拿到完整非线性飞机上压力测试一遍,才能说它"能用"。

## 故障叙事

本级每个实验都以同一个动作收尾:注入标准故障库里的一种——四旋翼这边是电机推力衰减或卡死,FT-1 这边是升降舵部分失效或副翼卡死——记录发生了什么。重点不是让 PID 出丑,而是给“鲁棒裕度”这个词一个学生亲手测出来的精确基线——这样当 L3 引入滑模控制去对付同一批故障时,那份提升是他们自己测量出来的,而不是课件上的一句断言。
跨入状态空间。为 L1 里那两种机体分别设计 LQR 调节器,并估计那些你无法直接测量的状态。
本科三年级 / 硕士入门
状态空间表示、线性代数
在 FT-1 上处理配平、纵向动力学与横航向耦合——这次用的是状态空间形式,不再是 L1 那套频域 PID 够用的表示。设计一个纵向 LQR 控制器,拿去和 L1 里固定翼的 PID 级联,在同一个非线性模型测试上直接对比。
估计 FT-1 测不到的状态
为 FT-1 上无法直接测量的状态搭建一个 Luenberger 观测器——这正是 L5 中残差生成、进而实现故障检测的同一个思路,这里先在固定翼的状态空间模型上做一遍。
在已知位置的舵面失效下稳住飞机
把一侧副翼卡死在一个已知的偏转角上,看它彻底退出可控输入集——不是打折,是没了。检查剩下那侧副翼(现在是独立的,不再靠一个耦合的差动输入捆在一起)还能不能让降阶后的系统保持可控,围绕它重新设计 LQR 增益,然后会发现纯状态反馈消不掉一个稳态偏置——这个缺口正是引入积分作用的动机,也是 L5 用主动重构去补上的那个开口。
把线性化的四轴模型重新写成状态空间形式,建立哪些状态直接可控、哪些相互耦合的物理直觉。
调节 Q/R 权重矩阵,亲身感受状态偏差代价与控制能量代价之间的取舍,并与 L1 的 PID 在同一套故障上直接对比。
为四轴上无法直接测量的状态引入观测器(Luenberger 型)——这正是 L5 中残差生成、进而实现故障检测的同一个思路。

L2 是 PID 与后续更一般控制理论之间刻意设置的桥梁:这是从传递函数跨入状态空间表示的自然一步,在 L1 已经引入的那两种机体上继续往下走。

## 一套理论,两架飞行器

学生要为四轴(以悬停为中心、结构对称)和 FT-1 固定翼测试平台(以配平为中心、纵向/横航向分离)设计同一套 LQR 框架。看到同一套控制理论应用在两种物理上完全不同的飞行器上,比记住任何一道标准答案都更有价值。

## 故障叙事的下一步

同一套标准故障库再次出现——四轴这边是电机推力衰减,FT-1 这边是升降舵/副翼故障——但这一次讨论的重点变了:LQR 的多回路联调可能在主轴上优于 PID,却可能悄悄让 PID 从未触及的耦合轴性能变差。这种对模型的依赖性,正是 L3 滑模控制要解决的弱点。
从 Lyapunov 论证出发设计滑模面,亲手在真实执行器上感受抖振,并测出 PID 与 LQR 都给不出的鲁棒性。
Lyapunov 稳定性、非线性系统
从 Lyapunov 到滑模面,在 FT-1 上
为 FT-1 的姿态动力学推导滑模面——同样是误差及其导数的组合,同样证明到达条件、实时看轨迹收敛——但这次对象是一套跟四轴截然不同的动力学。
FT-1 执行器上的趋近律与抖振
在升降舵和副翼舵机上对比切换、指数、幂次趋近律。把抖振感受为真实的执行器磨损,不是课本注脚,然后用边界层平滑,亲眼看它消失。
完整的 FT-1 纵向滑模控制器,对气动系数不确定性保持鲁棒——在同一套固定翼故障集上,跟 L1 的 PID、L2 的 LQR 直接基准对比。
对抗一个卡在未知位置的舵面故障
跟 L2 一样的副翼卡死故障,但更难:这次卡死的角度未知,只知道一个界。证明只要剩下的控制权限还压得住这个界,滑模面依然能被到达——这是学生要亲手验证的条件,不是假设出来的——而 L2 的 LQR 只能处理一个已经知道精确大小的故障。
把误差及其导数组合成滑模面,证明到达条件,实时观察轨迹如何收敛到滑模面上。
对比切换、指数、幂次趋近律。在硬件上把抖振感受为真实的执行器磨损——而不是课本上的一段话——然后用边界层/饱和函数平滑,亲眼看它消失。
完整的姿态滑模控制器,在与 L1/L2 完全相同的故障集上进行基准对比。
逐级升级故障下的鲁棒性
电机推力衰减三级递增(60% → 40% → 25%)。在 PID/LQR 已经放弃的档位,SMC 依然维持跟踪——这是测出来的,不是断言的。

L3 是关于鲁棒性的第一课。在此之前的每一级都假设模型是准确的。L3 从相反的假设出发——模型在一定范围内是错的——追问一个控制器在这种情况下究竟还能保证什么。

## 硬件让这个取舍变得真实

抖振通常只是滑模控制课堂上的一句注脚。而在真实执行器、真实开关频率下,它是伺服机构在自我磨损时发出的声音和热量。学生亲手调节边界层平滑参数、亲耳听到差异,这让“鲁棒性 vs. 控制信号质量”这个经典权衡,变成他们自己权衡过的东西,而不是读到过的东西。

## 逐级升级故障演示

本级的招牌演示分两种,一个机体一种,而且升级的方向不一样。四轴这边,同一个电机衰减故障设置成三个递增严重度(60% → 40% → 25%),把 PID、LQR、SMC 并排对比——到最后一档,只有 SMC 还在跟踪。FT-1 这边,升级的不是严重度,是确定性:L2 的 LQR 处理的是一个卡死位置已知的副翼故障;L3 的 SMC 处理同一个卡死故障,但位置变成未知、只知道界。两边都不是"SMC 赢了"这么一句口号——重点是这份鲁棒性的代价(控制信号抖振)能在示波器上看见,而不是写在课件里被断言。
L4 — 从增益调度到 LPV
发现为什么单一固定控制器在整个飞行包线内是不够的,然后用有理论保证的 LPV 控制器取代手工增益调度。
线性矩阵不等式(LMI)、时变参数系统稳定性
为什么一个控制器不够用
把单一工作点设计的 LQR/SMC 推向大范围飞行包线——四轴大角度机动,或固定翼大空速范围——观察性能如何退化直至失稳。
在多个工作点设计 LQR 增益并手工插值,构造一个'点间过渡失稳'案例:每个设计点都稳定,插值出的中间系统却不稳定。
围绕调度参数(空速/姿态角)搭建多胞体(polytopic)LPV 模型,理解顶点系统的物理含义,运行平台提供的 MATLAB 脚本完成离线 LMI 综合。
把调度增益的 LPV 控制器烧录到 STM32,拿它去对付前面击垮经典增益调度的同一个点间过渡案例——这一次它稳住了。

L4 的存在是为了纠正一个控制工程里不知不觉养成的习惯:相信一个在若干工作点调好的控制器,在这些点之间也一定管用。事实往往不是这样,本级课程的设计,就是先让这个失效清清楚楚地发生,再去正确地解决它。

## 一个刻意设计的失效场景

L4 的招牌实验刻意构造出一个过渡失稳案例:若干个各自独立看都稳定、调得很好的工作点,用工程师一直以来插值增益的方式连起来,中间却跑出一条不稳定的轨迹。亲眼看着自己调得很好的增益以这种方式失效,正是 LPV 方案落地的关键一刻——一份有 LMI 支撑的正式保证,取代了一个你已经不能完全信任的插值。

## 离线设计,在线执行

本级第一次把“离线设计 vs. 在线执行”这条边界讲清楚:LMI 综合在实验课开始之前就已经在 MATLAB 里离线完成;真正在 STM32 上实时运行的,只是针对调度参数的增益插值。这条边界——哪些东西是离线保证好的,哪些只是在线执行——正是每一个嵌入式控制工程师都要内化的东西,也是 L5 直接依赖的基础。
平台的旗舰级课程:闭合故障检测、隔离与主动重构的完整环路,把 L1-L4 的全部工具汇聚成一个真正能工作的容错飞控系统。
硕士毕业设计 / 博士 / 科研训练
L1-L4 全部内容的综合运用
基于 L2 的观测器构建残差生成器,用 ROC 式分析把检测阈值设计成误报与漏报之间真实的取舍,并在完整标准故障库上对比检测延迟与准确率——四轴这边隔离出是哪个电机,FT-1 这边隔离出是哪一侧副翼。
被动容错 vs. 主动容错
把 L3 的滑模控制器——被动、不检测、纯靠鲁棒性——与 FDI 加重构的主动策略在同一批故障上正面对比。这个取舍是真实的:被动方案简单、保守;主动方案性能更高,但要承担检测延迟与误判的风险。
把故障严重程度本身变成 LPV 的调度参数之一。FDI 输出的健康度估计实时重新调度控制器增益;滑模控制则吸收尚未被识别出来的不确定性。这是平台自有的、源自博士研究的方法,不是课本上的现成配方。
飞行中单电机完全失效
四轴飞行机动过程中一个电机完全失效。学生亲手设计控制分配矩阵的重构,看着四旋翼的姿态控制权限退化为一个可控、但能力削弱的三电机模式。
在 FT-1 上绕开一侧卡死的副翼
FT-1 左右独立的副翼,加上升降舵本身的对称/差动两种模式,给了控制分配不止一条恢复滚转权限的路。FDI 的健康度估计判断出是哪一侧副翼卡死;加权控制分配实时把滚转权限重新分配到还健康的那侧副翼和差动升降舵模式上。同一个卡死故障,L2 是在已知位置下对付它、L3 是在未知位置下对付它——到这里,靠检测加重构接过了被动鲁棒性交出的答案。
毕业项目:随机故障任务
一次完整飞行任务中,随机注入 2-3 个故障(类型、时刻、严重程度均随机)。学生完整的 FDI+重构方案会依据任务完成度被自动评分——这同时也是平台课程竞赛的形式。

L5 是平台不再单独教工具、而开始教这些工具究竟为了什么而存在的地方:一个完整的、真正能工作的容错飞控闭环。这也是唯一一级直接来自创始人本人博士期间 LPV/SMC 容错飞控研究的课程——是这套课程体系里,任何一本通用控制教材、以及市面上任何其他教学平台都给不出的那一部分。

## 让核心工程取舍变得可见

被动容错(一个从不寻找故障、只靠鲁棒性硬扛的控制器)和主动容错(先检测、再重构)通常被当作一个已有定论的问题呈现。L5 不给出定论——它让学生把两者放在同一批故障上跑一遍,让数据自己说话:被动方案保守但简单、没有检测延迟;主动方案可能表现更好,但要承担误报和漏报的风险。这里没有一个放之四海皆准的正确答案,实验本身的设计,就是要让学生自己发现这一点。

## 毕业项目

从 L1 起积累的每一件工具——级联回路、状态空间设计、基于 Lyapunov 的鲁棒性、LMI 保证的增益调度——都在这里汇聚成一个控制器,而它要面对的,是一个它事先并不知道故障脚本的真实任务。这个设计刻意呈现为课程竞赛的形式,也是这个平台对每一个容错控制研究团队迟早都会问新生的那个问题给出的答案:**现在,把它们全部装到一起。**
Five progressive controller levels on real hardware: PID, LQR, sliding mode control, gain scheduling, and active fault tolerance, on quadrotor and fixed-wing airframes side by side.

Build the flight-control intuition: design and tune a cascaded PID attitude controller on both a fixed-wing and a quadrotor airframe, and see why the two of them get there by genuinely different paths.
Build and verify the airframe model

Build FT-1's aerodynamic model in AVL from its real geometry, then trim and linearize it in Simulink. Cross-check the linearization against a second, independent method before trusting a single number out of it — every controller in this level stands on this model.

Design a straightforward attitude-only PID — pitch and roll each closing directly on elevator or aileron — and tune it against the linear model with pidtune. It mostly works, until you look closely: one axis rings for tens of seconds after every command and never quite settles, no matter how the single gain is retuned.

Work out why retuning the single loop can't fix the ringing: its correction reaches the aircraft's rate only after an extra integration, adding phase lag that excites a lightly-damped mode instead of damping it. Rebuild as an inner rate loop plus an outer attitude loop and watch steady-state error and ringing both collapse to a few percent of the command.
Test the linear design against the nonlinear aircraft

The cascade was designed entirely on a small-signal linear model. Run the same gains closed-loop against FT-1's full nonlinear dynamics and find out how much of that clean performance survives the assumptions the linearization made.
Fly with a control surface degraded

Inject a partial elevator or a jammed aileron mid-maneuver and record what the classical PID cascade does with a fault it was never designed to detect. This is the baseline every later level's fault-tolerant design gets measured against.
Characterize and verify the airframe model

Build a rigid-body-plus-motor model from bench-measured thrust and torque coefficients — no aerodynamic panel code here, a quadrotor's model comes from characterizing hardware directly. Verify it against real thrust-stand data before trusting it for control design.
Design the inner angular-rate PID. Feel bandwidth, overshoot, and steady-state error as flight-relevant vocabulary, not textbook abstractions.

A quadrotor doesn't get the fixed-wing's luxury of trying attitude-only control and watching it merely underperform — with no aerodynamic self-damping, an attitude loop with no rate feedback underneath it is a real crash risk, not a ringing curve on a plot. Work out why analytically first, then add the outer attitude loop on real hardware already knowing why it has to be there.
Test the linear design against the nonlinear airframe

The cascade was tuned on a small-angle linearization. Push it into large-angle manoeuvres where the Euler-angle and gyroscopic-coupling nonlinearities the linear model ignored start to matter, and see where the margins actually are.
Inject 60% single-motor thrust loss and an IMU noise burst live. Record exactly how far PID can stretch before it loses stability — and where L3/L5 pick up the slack.

Every controller level in Robavionix runs against the same standard fault library. L1 is where that story starts: a classical cascaded PID attitude controller, tuned by hand on two physically different airframes, pushed until each one breaks in its own way.

## Why start here

Most control-education failures aren't about content depth — they're about students crossing a math threshold without ever touching a real, verified plant. L1 assumes only transfer functions and frequency response. No state space, no Lyapunov functions. The goal is a working controller inside the first lab session on both airframes, and a precise, felt answer to a question that turns out to have two different answers: **how far can a PID controller be pushed before it gives up — and does that limit look the same on a quadrotor as it does on a fixed-wing aircraft?**

## What you build

On the quadrotor: a rate-loop PID on each axis, then an outer attitude loop cascaded on top, deployed to the STM32 flight controller board and exercised against the physical quadrotor testbed inside the HIL rig — not a pure simulation. On FT-1: the same rate + attitude cascade structure, designed against an AVL/Simulink aerodynamic model that's cross-checked two independent ways before a single gain gets tuned against it, then pressure-tested against the full nonlinear aircraft before anything gets called "working."

## The fault story

Every experiment in this level ends the same way: inject a fault from the standard library — degraded or jammed motor thrust on the quadrotor, a partial elevator or a jammed aileron on FT-1 — and record what happens. The point isn't to make PID fail dramatically — it's to give students a **precise, hands-on baseline** for the phrase "robustness margin," so that when L3 introduces sliding mode control against the same faults, the improvement is something they measured themselves, not something a lecture slide told them was true.
Cross into state-space control. Design an LQR regulator for both airframes from L1, and estimate the states you can't measure directly.

Trim, longitudinal dynamics, and coupled roll-yaw on FT-1, now handled in the state-space form L1's frequency-response PID didn't need. Design an LQR longitudinal controller and compare it directly against L1's fixed-wing PID cascade on the same nonlinear-model test.
Build a Luenberger observer for FT-1's states that aren't directly sensed — the same residual-generation idea that becomes fault detection in L5, worked out here on the fixed-wing state-space model.
Stabilize through a known actuator loss

Jam one aileron at a known deflection and watch it drop out of the controllable input set entirely — not degraded, gone. Check whether the surviving aileron (independent now, not coupled through one differential input) keeps the reduced system controllable, redesign the LQR gain around it, and find the steady-state offset pure state feedback can't null. That gap is what motivates integral action — and it's the opening L5 walks through with active reconfiguration.
Rebuild the linearised quadrotor model in state-space form and build physical intuition for which states are directly actuated and which are coupled.
Tune the Q/R weighting matrices and feel the state-error-vs-control-effort trade-off directly, compared side by side against L1's PID on the same fault set.
Introduce an observer (Luenberger-style) for quadrotor states you can't measure directly — the same residual-generation idea that becomes fault detection in L5.

L2 is the deliberate bridge between PID and the more general control theory that follows: the natural jump from transfer functions to state-space representation, worked through on the same two airframes L1 already introduced.

## Two controllers, one theory

Students design the same LQR framework for both the quadrotor (hover-centric, symmetric) and FT-1, the fixed-wing testbed (trim-centric, longitudinal/lateral-directional split). Seeing one control theory applied to two physically different vehicles is worth more than any amount of correct answers memorised in isolation.

## Where the fault story goes next

The same standard fault library returns — motor thrust loss on the quadrotor, elevator/aileron faults on FT-1 — but now the discussion is different: LQR's multi-loop tuning can outperform PID on the primary axis while quietly degrading performance on cross-axes the single-loop PID never touched. That model-dependence is exactly the weakness L3's sliding mode control is built to answer.
Design a sliding surface from a Lyapunov argument, feel chattering with your own hands on real actuators, and measure robustness that PID and LQR can't match.
From Lyapunov to a sliding surface, on FT-1

Derive a sliding surface for FT-1's attitude dynamics as a combination of error and its derivative, prove reachability, and watch the trajectory converge onto it in real time — the same derivation as the quadrotor track, worked against a genuinely different set of dynamics.
Reaching law & chattering on FT-1's actuators

Compare switching, exponential, and power-rate reaching laws on the elevator and aileron servos. Feel chattering as real actuator wear, not a textbook footnote, then apply boundary-layer smoothing and watch it disappear.
Full sliding-mode longitudinal controller for FT-1, robust against aerodynamic-coefficient uncertainty — benchmarked against L1's PID and L2's LQR on the same fixed-wing fault set.
Robustness against an actuator jammed at an unknown position

Same jammed-aileron fault as L2, made harder: the stuck deflection is now unknown, only bounded. Show the sliding surface still gets reached as long as the remaining control authority still dominates that bound — a condition students check, not assume — where L2's LQR could only handle a fault it already knew the exact size of.
Derive a sliding surface as a combination of error and its derivative, prove reachability, and watch the trajectory converge onto it in real time.

Compare switching, exponential, and power-rate reaching laws. Feel chattering as physical actuator wear on hardware — not a paragraph in a textbook — then apply boundary-layer / saturation smoothing and watch it disappear.
Full sliding mode attitude controller, benchmarked against L1/L2 on the identical fault set.
Motor thrust degraded across three severities (60% → 40% → 25%). SMC keeps tracking after PID/LQR have already given up — measured, not asserted.

L3 is the first lesson in robustness. Everything before this level assumed the model was correct. L3 starts from the opposite assumption — the model is wrong, within bounds — and asks what a controller can guarantee anyway.

## The hardware makes the tradeoff real

Chattering is usually a footnote in a sliding-mode lecture. On real actuators at real switching frequencies, it's the sound and heat of a servo working itself to death. Students tune boundary-layer smoothing and hear the difference, which makes the classic "robustness vs. control-signal-quality" tradeoff something they've traded off themselves, not something they've read about.

## The escalating-fault demonstration

This level's signature demonstration comes in two flavours, one per airframe, and they escalate in different directions. On the quadrotor, the same motor-degradation fault runs at three increasing severities (60% → 40% → 25%) with PID, LQR, and SMC lined up side by side — SMC is the only one still tracking at the last stage. On FT-1, the escalation isn't severity, it's certainty: L2's LQR handles a jammed aileron whose stuck position is known; L3's SMC handles the same jam with that position now unknown, only bounded. Neither case is "SMC wins" as a slogan — the point is that the cost of that robustness (control-signal chatter) is visible on an oscilloscope, not asserted in a slide.
Discover why a single fixed controller isn't enough across the flight envelope, then replace hand-tuned gain scheduling with a formally-guaranteed LPV controller.
Linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), stability of parameter-varying systems
Push a single-point LQR/SMC design across a large flight envelope — big-angle quadrotor manoeuvres, or fixed-wing airspeed range — and watch performance degrade toward instability.
Design LQR gains at several operating points and interpolate by hand. Construct a point-to-point transition instability case: every design point is stable, the interpolated system in between is not.

Build a polytopic LPV model over the scheduling parameters (airspeed / attitude), understand the physical meaning of the vertex systems, and run the offline LMI synthesis provided by the platform's MATLAB scripts.
Flash the scheduled-gain LPV controller to STM32 and run it against classical gain scheduling on the same point-to-point transition case that broke it earlier — this time it holds.

L4 exists to correct a habit most control engineering builds without meaning to: the belief that a controller tuned to work well at several points must work everywhere in between. It usually doesn't, and this level is built around making that failure visible before it's fixed properly.

## A deliberately staged failure

The signature experiment in L4 constructs a transition-instability case by design: several individually stable, well-tuned operating points, interpolated in the way engineers have always interpolated gains, producing an unstable trajectory in between. Watching your own correctly-tuned gains fail this way is what makes the LPV alternative land — a formal, LMI-backed guarantee replaces an interpolation you can no longer fully trust.

## Offline design, online execution

This level makes the offline-design-versus-online-execution boundary explicit for the first time: the LMI synthesis runs offline in MATLAB before the lab session; what actually executes on STM32 in real time is nothing more than gain interpolation against the scheduling parameter. That boundary — what's guaranteed offline, what merely executes online — is the same one every embedded control engineer has to internalise, and L5 depends on it directly.
The platform's signature level: close the loop on fault detection, isolation, and active reconfiguration, combining every tool from L1-L4 into one working fault-tolerant flight controller.

Build a residual generator from the L2 observer, design the detection threshold as a genuine trade-off between false alarms and missed detections using ROC-style analysis, and benchmark detection delay and accuracy against the full standard fault library — isolating which motor on the quadrotor, which aileron on FT-1.

Compare L3's sliding-mode controller — passive, no detection, pure robustness — head to head against an FDI-plus-reconfiguration active strategy on the same faults. The trade-off is real: passive is simple and conservative, active is higher-performing but exposed to detection delay and misclassification risk.

Fold fault severity itself into the LPV scheduling parameter. FDI's health-estimate output retunes controller gain in real time; sliding mode absorbs whatever uncertainty hasn't yet been identified. This is the platform's own PhD-derived method, not a textbook recipe.

Complete loss of one motor on the quadrotor mid-manoeuvre. Students design the control-allocation matrix reconfiguration by hand and watch four-rotor attitude authority degrade into a controllable, if reduced, three-motor mode.
Reconfigure around a jammed aileron on FT-1

FT-1's independent left and right ailerons, plus the elevator's own symmetric/differential split, give the allocator more than one lever for roll authority. FDI's health estimate identifies which aileron jammed; weighted control allocation redistributes roll authority across the surviving aileron and the differential-elevator mode in real time. Same jam L2 fought with a known position and L3 fought with an unknown one — this is where detection plus reconfiguration replaces passive robustness as the answer.

One flight mission, 2-3 randomized faults (type, timing, severity) injected mid-run. Students' complete FDI-plus-reconfiguration stack is scored automatically against mission completion. This doubles as the platform's course-competition format.

L5 is where the platform stops teaching individual tools and starts teaching the thing they were always for: a complete, working fault-tolerant flight control loop. It is also the level that comes directly out of the founder's own PhD research on LPV/SMC fault-tolerant flight control — the one piece of this curriculum that a generic controls textbook, and every other teaching platform on the market, simply doesn't have.

## The core engineering trade-off, made visible

Passive fault tolerance (a robust controller that never looks for faults) and active fault tolerance (detect, then reconfigure) are usually presented as a settled question. L5 doesn't settle it — it has students run both against the same faults and let the data make the argument: passive is conservative but simple and detection-delay-free; active can perform better but is exposed to false alarms and missed detections. There is no universally correct answer, and the experiment is built so students discover that themselves.

## The capstone

Every tool accumulated since L1 — cascaded loops, state-space design, Lyapunov-based robustness, LMI-guaranteed scheduling — converges here into one controller that has to survive a mission it hasn't seen the fault schedule for in advance. It is, deliberately, shaped like a course competition, and it is the platform's answer to the question every fault-tolerant-control research group eventually asks a new student: **now put it all together.**
Robavionix 是一个容错飞控 HIL 教学平台。真实的 STM32 硬件,真实的 Simulink 模型,一个故障注入开关——设计一个控制器,然后现场看着它失效、再恢复。

Robavionix is a fault-tolerant flight control HIL teaching platform. Real STM32 hardware, real Simulink models, and a fault-injection switch — design a controller, then watch it fail and recover, live.
Robavionix 源自一个人的博士研究——仿生变形翼无人机的容错飞控——不是某个委员会白板会议的产物。

我最早的梦想,是当一个汽车设计师。结果整个本科,流体力学和自动控制,是我最差的两门课。

我是那种"哪门最差、就偏要跟哪门死磕"的人。所以后来这些年,我几乎每一次选择,都是主动挑自己最不会的那一块下手——Robavionix 说到底,就是这个毛病一路发展到最后的样子。

大三毕设,我一头扎进 CFD,从零建了一整套涡轮机的定子和转子叶片;硕士读的是航空发动机方向,毕设又选了附面层吸收——纯手推附面层那部分网格,再用 CFD 去算它损耗掉的能量。每一次挑的,都是当时最不擅长的流体。等到读博定题,空气动力学、推进这些我已经摸得差不多了,唯一还剩一整块空白的,是航电和飞控。于是我又一次,选了自己最不会的那个。

## 看不见的控制器

这一次比前几次都狠。MATLAB 不熟,飞控从零,我基本是和一群大二学生坐在一起,用一年时间硬啃本该好几年才补得完的底子。

但真正折磨我的不是难,是"看不见"。第一次学 PID,我盯着一个弹簧振子,怎么都想不明白 P、I、D 这三个东西到底是怎么合力把它拉回去的——最后是我自己动手搭了一个能看见的模型,才算真的懂了。后来做滑模控制,smooth factor 又遇到了同样的情况:一个只活在公式里、在脑子里怎么都立不起来的参数。

可我的无人机从来不是这样。我能在 AVL 里实打实地看见那架飞机,实打实地看见它每一个气动参数。空气动力学是看得见的,控制器却始终像个鬼影。

后来我站到了讲台的另一头。做控制工程助教的那几年,我每年带一百五十多个本科生,一遍遍看着他们撞上我当年撞过的同一堵墙:Lyapunov 稳定性证明的每一步都背得出来,却从没亲眼见过一个控制器真正工作、或者真正失效是什么样子。

试卷上那套设计流程、调参过程、那一串数字——再漂亮,也抵不过手里一个真的控制器、一个真的传感器,和屏幕上一架真的在飞、在机动、在失效边缘挣扎的飞机。

## 于是有了 Robavionix

我的博士做了四年多,主题是仿生变形翼无人机的容错飞控;那套 LPV/SMC 的方法在 IFAC 和 IEEE 上发表过四次,也就是今天课程里 [L5](/curriculum/l5-lpv-smc-fdi/) 的直接来源。它本身是纯理论——但既然从建模、到仿真、再到控制器设计这条路我已经完整走通了,我就不甘心让它停在纸上。

Robavionix 就是把这整条路搬到真实硬件上,让学生亲眼看见控制器怎么运作、怎么应对失效。不是委员会在白板上想出来的,是一个人一行一行、一次失效一次失效啃出来的。在真的板子上,安全地——想失败几次,就失败几次。

Robavionix comes out of one person's PhD research on fault-tolerant flight control for morphing-wing UAVs — not a committee's whiteboard session.

I set out to become a car designer. Instead, fluid dynamics and control theory turned out to be my two worst subjects in the whole degree.

I'm the kind of person who, when a subject is my weakest, decides that's the exact one to go head-to-head with. So almost every choice I made afterwards was a deliberate step toward the thing I was worst at — and Robavionix, in the end, is that habit taken to its conclusion.

For my undergraduate thesis I threw myself at CFD, building a full turbine stator and rotor stage from scratch. For my master's I studied aero-engines, and picked a thesis on boundary-layer ingestion — deriving that part of the boundary-layer mesh by hand, then using CFD to compute the energy it bled away. Every time, I chose the fluids problem I was least comfortable with. By the time I came to choose a PhD, aerodynamics and propulsion were things I finally had a handle on — and the one area still completely blank was avionics and flight control. So once again, I picked the thing I was worst at.

## The controller you can't see

This time was harder than any that came before. I barely knew MATLAB, I'd never touched flight control, and I essentially sat in with a room full of second-year undergraduates, spending a year cramming foundations that should have taken several.

But what really got to me wasn't the difficulty — it was that none of it was visible. The first time I learned PID, I'd stare at a spring-mass system and simply could not picture how P, I and D actually pulled it back into place. In the end I had to build a model I could see before it truly clicked. Later, with sliding mode control, the smooth factor was the same kind of thing: a parameter that lived only in the equations and refused to take shape in my head.

My bio-inspired UAV were never like that. In AVL I could see the aircraft, plainly — I could see every one of its aerodynamic parameters. The aerodynamics were visible; the controller stayed a ghost.

Then I ended up on the other side of the lecture hall. For several years as a control-engineering teaching assistant I took 150+ undergraduates a year, and watched them hit, over and over, the exact wall I'd hit myself: able to recite every step of a Lyapunov stability proof, yet having never once seen a controller actually work — or actually fail.

The design procedure on the exam paper, the tuning, the string of numbers — however elegant, none of it beats a real controller in your hand, a real sensor, and a real aircraft on the screen flying, manoeuvring, fighting to stay in control at the edge of failure.

## So Robavionix exists

My PhD ran for over four years, on fault-tolerant flight control for bio-inspired, morphing-wing UAVs; the LPV/SMC work behind it was peer-reviewed and presented four times at IFAC and IEEE conferences, and it is the direct source of [L5](/curriculum/l5-lpv-smc-fdi/) in the curriculum. It was pure theory — but having built the whole path once, from modelling to simulation to controller design, I couldn't bring myself to leave it on paper.

Robavionix moves that entire path onto real hardware, so students can watch a controller work, and watch it handle failure. Not something a committee drew on a whiteboard — something one person built line by line, failure by failure. On real boards, safely — failing as many times as it takes.

Questions about the research behind a specific level, or how it maps to your syllabus? [Get in touch](/#contact).

Built from a PhD in fault-tolerant flight control
Robavionix turns one researcher's LPV/SMC fault-tolerant flight control work into hardware students can actually fail, fix, and learn from.
Every level in the curriculum runs on real STM32 hardware with a fault-injection switch, so the gap between "reading about robustness" and "measuring it yourself" closes on day one.

See a controller fail.
Then fix it.

Robavionix is a fault-tolerant flight control HIL teaching platform. Real STM32 hardware, real Simulink models, and a fault-injection switch — so students design a controller, then watch it lose stability and recover, live.
Bring fault-tolerant control into your course
From a single Core kit for self-study to a full Classroom set for ten students, we'll help you find the right fit for your syllabus.

Not a simulation-only toy. Real hardware, real faults
Every controller you design gets flashed to an STM32 board and exercised against a physical testbed inside a HIL rig — with a fault-injection switch built into the hardware, not bolted on afterward.
From transfer functions to Lyapunov-based robustness, each level closes with the same question: inject a standard fault and measure exactly where the controller stops holding.

Every experiment follows
the same five-stage structure
Design and tune your controller entirely in a pure Simulink environment first — no hardware risk while you're still getting the theory right.
Auto-generate code and flash it to the real flight-controller board. The same model now runs against a physical testbed inside the HIL rig.
Trigger a standard fault live, record how the controller responds, and get an auto-generated performance report against a clear pass threshold.
